articles.title: Heywood School Linking Event Engages Pupils with M6 Theatre
articles.excerpt: Pupils in Heywood recently participated in a school linking event organised by the council in collaboration with M6 Theatre, which featured engaging workshops.
articles.content_html:
A school linking event took place in Heywood recently, where pupils participated in workshops led by teams from the council and M6 Theatre. The event aimed to foster collaboration and creativity among students.

The workshops were designed to be engaging and fun, providing pupils with an opportunity to work closely with theatre professionals. This initiative is part of a broader effort to enhance educational experiences through the arts.

Participants expressed enthusiasm for the activities, which included various interactive sessions that encouraged teamwork and artistic expression. The collaboration between the council and M6 Theatre highlights the importance of community involvement in education.

This event is expected to have a positive impact on the pupils, as it promotes not only artistic skills but also social interaction and teamwork among students from different schools.
articles.image_credit: Andrew Purdie from Oldham, Greater Manchester, England / CC BY 2.0 via Wikimedia Commons

articles.title: Heywood Students Visit First Choice Homes Oldham as Part of Summer School
articles.excerpt: Three teenagers from the Heywood and Middleton North constituency participated in a two-day programme with First Choice Homes Oldham, gaining insights into the housing association's operations.
articles.content_html:
Three teenagers from the Heywood and Middleton North constituency spent two days with First Choice Homes Oldham as part of MP Elsie Blundell's Fly High Summer School. Emaan Hassan, Caryn-Ann Sarpong-Kumankomah and Charlie Wilcock, all aged 16, were mentored by chief executive Mark Gifford and members of the senior leadership team.

During their visit, the students engaged with various departments, including development, neighbourhood services, sustainability, and the customer contact centre. This experience provided them with insights into the work of the housing association and its role in supporting communities across Oldham.

First Choice Homes Oldham also donated £300 to the Fly High Summer School to support the group's visit to the Houses of Parliament in London. Reflecting on the experience, Emaan Hassan noted the size of the organisation and the number of employees involved. Caryn-Ann Sarpong-Kumankomah found the meeting with the chief executive particularly beneficial, while Charlie Wilcock expressed surprise at the scale of social housing managed by the organisation.

Mark Gifford stated that the organisation was pleased to host the students and highlighted the importance of such initiatives in providing young people with insights into local democracy. MP Elsie Blundell expressed gratitude for the warm welcome the students received and emphasised the value of partnerships in creating opportunities for young people.
articles.legal_disclaimer: This article was compiled from identified public sources and may be updated when further verified information becomes available.

articles.title: Meeting Requested to Discuss Hopwood Triangle Road Closure
articles.excerpt: A meeting has been requested with the Highways team and local councillors to address the impact of the Hopwood Triangle road closure in Heywood.
articles.content_html:
A meeting has been requested with the Highways team, including all councillors in Heywood, to discuss the Hopwood Triangle scheme. The request aims to address the significant impact the associated road closure has had on the town.

The closure has raised concerns among local residents and businesses, prompting the need for a collaborative discussion on the matter. The meeting is expected to focus on the implications of the road closure and potential solutions.

Details regarding the date and time of the meeting have not yet been confirmed. However, it is anticipated that the discussions will involve various stakeholders affected by the changes in traffic flow.

Further updates will be provided as more information becomes available regarding the meeting and any decisions made thereafter.

articles.title: Rochdale Council Plans Crackdown on Houses of Multiple Occupation
articles.excerpt: Rochdale Council is set to introduce tighter controls on houses of multiple occupation (HMOs) following recent protests in Heywood organised by Jamie Thomason
articles.content_html:
ONGOING STORY — Updated 19 July 2026 at 06:28. 3 source updates have been combined into this article.

Rochdale Council is set to introduce tighter controls on houses of multiple occupation (HMOs) following recent protests in Heywood organised by Jamie Thomason

Latest update

Rochdale Council is preparing to implement stricter regulations on houses of multiple occupation (HMOs) in the borough. This decision follows recent protests against HMOs, particularly in Heywood, where residents have raised issues regarding their impact on local services and community cohesion.

Reform councillors have urged town hall officials to introduce an Article 4 Direction, which would require all HMOs, regardless of size, to obtain specific planning permission. Currently, only HMOs accommodating seven or more people are subject to this requirement. Councillor Paul O’Neill stated that the council aims to alleviate tensions surrounding HMOs in the area.

Councillor Lee Wolf from the Reform group highlighted that while HMO licensing is crucial, it does not address the location of these properties or prevent excessive clustering. He noted that residents want a greater say in the matter, as many believe HMOs are negatively affecting their neighbourhoods.

The Labour group, which currently controls the council, has indicated that it is already working on changes to HMO regulations. Housing boss Councillor Danny Meredith acknowledged the support from Reform for the Labour plan, while Councillor Richard Jackson emphasised the need to follow proper processes before implementing an Article 4 Direction. This directive is expected to be brought before the full council for a formal vote later this year.

Earlier developments

Rochdale Council is set to crack down on houses of multiple occupation (HMOs) in the borough. Reform councillors have called on town hall officials to implement stricter regulations. This decision follows protests against HMOs that took place over the weekend in Heywood.

Councillor Paul O’Neill stated that the council aims to introduce an Article 4 Direction to alleviate tensions surrounding HMOs. The issue was raised by Reform councillors during the latest town hall meeting. Article 4 directives would require all HMOs, regardless of size, to obtain specific planning permission. Currently, only HMOs accommodating seven or more people require such permission.

Councillor Lee Wolf from Reform expressed that while HMO licensing is important, it does not address the location of these properties or prevent excessive clustering. He noted that residents desire a greater say in the matter and believe that HMOs are impacting local services, particularly in specific areas.

The controlling Labour group indicated that it is already planning changes. Housing boss Councillor Danny Meredith thanked Reform for their support of the Labour plan. Fellow Labour councillor Richard Jackson added that they are actively working on the issue but must follow proper processes. He emphasised that implementing Article 4 requires substantial evidence and cannot be enacted immediately. The proposal is currently undergoing local authority processes and is expected to be presented to the full council for a formal vote later this year.

Update timeline

  • 19 July 2026, 06:28: Rochdale Council Plans Crackdown on Houses of Multiple Occupation
  • 18 July 2026, 07:43: Rochdale Council to Tighten Controls on Houses of Multiple Occupation

articles.excerpt: Rochdale Council is set to introduce tighter controls on houses of multiple occupation (HMOs) in the borough. This decision follows protests against HMOs that took place over the weekend in Heywood. Councillor Paul O’Neill…
articles.content_html:
Rochdale Council is set to introduce tighter controls on houses of multiple occupation (HMOs) in the borough. Reform councillors have called on town hall officials to implement an Article 4 Direction to address growing tensions surrounding HMOs. This decision follows protests against HMOs that took place over the weekend in Heywood.

Councillor Paul O’Neill stated that the council aims to ease tensions by ensuring that all HMOs, regardless of size, require specific planning permission. Currently, only HMOs accommodating seven or more people need such permission. Reform councillor Lee Wolf highlighted that while HMO licensing is important, it does not regulate their locations or prevent excessive clustering in certain areas.

Residents have expressed a desire for greater input regarding HMOs, with many believing they are negatively impacting local services. The Labour group, which currently controls the council, acknowledged the issue and indicated that they are already planning changes. Housing boss Councillor Danny Meredith thanked Reform for their support of the Labour plan.

Councillor Richard Jackson from Labour added that the council is actively working on this matter but emphasised the need to follow proper processes. He noted that implementing an Article 4 Direction requires substantial evidence and cannot be enacted immediately. The proposal is currently undergoing local authority processes and is expected to be presented to the full council for a formal vote later this year.

articles.title: Traffic Disruption in Heywood Due to Middleton Road Changes
articles.excerpt: Traffic in Heywood has been severely disrupted today, partly due to the ongoing pedestrianisation of Middleton Road, affecting routes throughout the town.
articles.content_html:
Traffic in Heywood has been severely disrupted today, partly due to the ongoing pedestrianisation of Middleton Road. Reports indicate that the changes are causing significant congestion across various routes in the town.

The alterations to Middleton Road are part of an experimental scheme aimed at improving pedestrian access. However, some residents have questioned the timing of the changes, particularly as other roadworks are also taking place in the area.

Witnesses described the situation as chaotic, with delays affecting not only the immediate vicinity of the 'Hopwood Triangle' but also extending throughout Heywood. The combination of roadworks and the new pedestrianisation measures has led to frustration among drivers and pedestrians alike.

Authorities have not yet commented on the situation or provided information regarding potential adjustments to the traffic management plan.

articles.title: Rochdale Youth Service Hosts Annual Youth Pride in the Park Event
articles.excerpt: Rochdale Youth Service's annual Youth Pride in the Park event attracted over 250 young attendees at Queen’s Park, Heywood, last week, featuring music, games, and activities.
articles.content_html:
Rochdale Youth Service held its annual Youth Pride in the Park event at Queen’s Park, Heywood, last week. The event saw more than 250 young people in attendance, enjoying a range of activities.

Participants enjoyed music, games, and various other activities throughout the evening, which was marked by pleasant weather. The event provided a vibrant atmosphere for attendees to engage with one another.

The event aims to celebrate diversity and promote inclusivity among young people in the community. It serves as an important platform for fostering connections and support among attendees.

This gathering allowed young people to connect and engage in a supportive environment, reinforcing the values of acceptance and community spirit.
